toggleHint: Show live DES metrics next to the scene (complements the static Learning Notes drawer)

eventClock.title: Event-driven clock
eventClock.summary: Time jumps from one event to the next instead of ticking continuously.
eventClock.friendlyHint: DES time doesn't tick like a stopwatch — it leaps straight to the next event. That's why fast-forward feels instant.
eventClock.friendlyFormula: Next jump = the earliest thing in the queue of upcoming events
eventClock.friendlyExpertDef: Imagine a to-do list of scheduled events, each with a planned time. The clock skips straight to the earliest one on the list — nothing in between needs computing.
eventClock.friendlyExpertWhy: Because idle time costs nothing, simulating an entire day of the café can finish in milliseconds.
eventClock.expertDef: The simulation clock advances to the earliest scheduled event. Between events nothing needs to be computed — idle time is effectively free.
eventClock.expertWhy: This is what lets DES scale: a model can simulate days in milliseconds as long as events stay sparse.

queueLength.title: Queue length L(t)
queueLength.summary: How long the line is right now, and how it grew over the run.
queueLength.friendlyFormula: Average queue length = add up the count at every moment ÷ total time
queueLength.friendlyExpertDef: Don't just look at "how many people right now" — look at "how many on average across the whole run". Add the queue size at every moment, divide by total time.
queueLength.friendlyExpertWhy: A rising curve means customers arrive faster than the shop can serve them. A falling curve means the shop is catching up.
queueLength.expertDef: L(t) is the number of customers in the system at time t. Its time-average L̄ is what Little's Law refers to.
queueLength.expertWhy: A rising L means arrivals outpace service; a falling L means the system is draining its backlog.

littlesLaw.title: Little's Law
littlesLaw.summary: A universal identity: long-run average customers equals arrival rate times average wait.
littlesLaw.friendlyEquation: {{lambda}} people flow in per minute × they stay {{w}} min ≈ {{l}} people in the cup
littlesLaw.friendlyFormula: People in the cup = arrivals per minute × minutes each one stays
littlesLaw.friendlyExpertDef: Picture a water cup: drops flow in from above (0.8 per minute), each drop stays a while (7 minutes), then drains out the bottom. The water level always equals "inflow × stay time".
littlesLaw.friendlyExpertWhy: It's a magical theorem: no matter how complex the shop's internals are, this relationship always holds. Measure any two and you get the third for free.
littlesLaw.expertDef: L, λ, and W are all time-averaged over a long-enough window. The identity holds for any stable queueing system regardless of distribution.
littlesLaw.expertWhy: Measure any two of L, λ, W and you get the third for free. It's the workhorse of operational analysis.

utilization.title: Utilization ρ
utilization.summary: How busy each resource is. The most-used one is the bottleneck.
utilization.friendlyHint: The busiest row is the bottleneck — expanding it is the only way to speed up the whole place.
utilization.friendlyFormula: Busyness = actual usage time ÷ total available time
utilization.friendlyExpertDef: Example: 10 seats open all day gives 10 × 24 = 240 seat-hours available. If customers actually used 180 of those hours, seat busyness = 180 ÷ 240 = 75%.
utilization.friendlyExpertWhy: Once busyness crosses 85%, even a small extra bump in arrivals blows the queue up. The only way to actually speed up the shop is to expand the busiest resource.
utilization.expertDef: ρ is the fraction of total capacity-time spent serving customers. For a multi-server station with c servers it's averaged across all c.
utilization.expertWhy: Once ρ passes ~0.85, small variations in arrivals cause disproportionate queue blowups. The bottleneck resource is where throughput improvements actually pay off.
